answers:
**1. Root cause of the cross-tenant leak: the application connects to Postgres as the schema-owning superuser (`cedar_owner`), and RLS does not apply to that role — so the isolation policies are silently skipped.**

The chain, with each link in a specific file:

- `cedarline/.env:1` (and `.env.example:1`) set `DATABASE_URL=postgresql://cedar_owner:ownerpw@localhost:5432/cedar`.
- `cedarline/backend/app/db.py:6` builds the SQLAlchemy engine directly from that `DATABASE_URL`, so every app connection is `cedar_owner`.
- `cedarline/docker-compose.yml:5` makes `cedar_owner` the `POSTGRES_USER` of the container. In the official Postgres image, `POSTGRES_USER` is the cluster **superuser**, and Postgres superusers bypass row-level security unconditionally.
- Independently, `cedarline/scripts/migrate.sh:4` runs all migrations as `cedar_owner`, so `cedar_owner` also **owns** the tables. `cedarline/db/migrations/0001_init.sql:21-27` uses `ALTER TABLE ... ENABLE ROW LEVEL SECURITY` but never `FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY` — and table owners bypass non-forced RLS. So even if `cedar_owner` were not a superuser, it would still bypass the policies.
- The endpoint depends entirely on RLS for filtering: `cedarline/backend/app/main.py:13` runs `SELECT id, tenant_id, name FROM clients` with no `WHERE tenant_id = ...`. The `SET app.current_tenant` in `cedarline/backend/app/deps.py:9-10` executes fine, but the `tenant_isolation` policy that would consume it (`0001_init.sql:22-23`) is never evaluated for a bypass-privileged connection. There is no error or warning — the policies just do nothing, and every tenant's rows (e.g. both seed tenants from `0001_init.sql:33-39`) come back.

The correctly-scoped role does exist in the design — `cedar_app` from `cedarline/db/bootstrap/create_app_role.sql` has only `SELECT/INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE` and no ownership or superuser bit — but nothing in the application ever uses it; only the test suite's default in `backend/tests/conftest.py:6` does. Git history confirms this was never wired up: `.env` has pointed at `cedar_owner` since the initial commit `a24f68f` (CED-1), and commit `03cd8a2` (CED-7) added `cedar_app` for the tests without repointing the app.

**2. The fix: connect the app as `cedar_app`, and keep two strictly separated roles going forward.**

- Change `DATABASE_URL` in `cedarline/.env` (and the template in `.env.example`) to `postgresql://cedar_app:apppw@localhost:5432/cedar`, so the engine in `backend/app/db.py` — and therefore `GET /clients` — runs as the least-privilege role that RLS actually applies to.
- Keep `cedar_owner` exclusively for schema changes: `scripts/migrate.sh` already hardcodes the owner URL, which is correct — migrations need ownership; the running app must never have it.
- As defense in depth, add `ALTER TABLE clients FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY;` (and the same for `appointments`) to the migrations, so even an accidental owner connection can't silently bypass the policies.
- Two adjacent weaknesses worth fixing at the same time: (a) `backend/app/deps.py` issues a session-level `SET app.current_tenant` on a connection drawn from SQLAlchemy's pool and `main.py` never closes it — the tenant setting can persist on the pooled connection and bleed into a later request for a different tenant; use `SET LOCAL` inside a transaction (or reset the GUC on connection check-in) instead. (b) The policy's `current_setting('app.current_tenant')` errors if the GUC was never set; `current_setting('app.current_tenant', true)` with a null-safe comparison fails closed more gracefully.
- Structurally going forward: application code paths get only the non-owning, non-superuser role with no `BYPASSRLS`; the owner role appears only in migration tooling; and an isolation test that queries as each tenant and asserts it sees only its own seed rows (the current `test_isolation.py:5-6` only asserts the URL contains `cedar_app`, which would not have caught this leak).

**3. The app role fails after a wipe because `cedar_app` lives in the cluster's role catalog inside the Docker volume that the reset script deletes, and nothing in the rebuild path recreates it.**

- Postgres roles are cluster-level objects stored in the data directory. `cedarline/docker-compose.yml:10-11` stores that data directory in the named volume `cedar_pgdata`.
- `cedarline/scripts/reset-local-db.sh:3` runs `docker compose down -v`, which removes that volume — destroying the entire cluster, including the `cedar_app` role. It then brings the container back up (a brand-new cluster containing only `cedar_owner`, recreated from the compose environment variables) and runs `./scripts/migrate.sh`, which only replays `db/migrations/*.sql` — schema and seed data, no roles.
- `cedar_app` is created solely by `cedarline/db/bootstrap/create_app_role.sql`, whose own header (lines 1–2) says it is a one-time manual bootstrap, "not part of automatic database initialization." `docs/local-setup.md` lists it only under "First time"; the "Reset your local db" section omits it entirely.
- The isolation suite's connection string defaults to `postgresql://cedar_app:apppw@localhost:5432/cedar` (`backend/tests/conftest.py:4-7`), so on the rebuilt cluster it fails immediately with Postgres's `FATAL: role "cedar_app" does not exist`. It "worked before the wipe" because the teammate had run the bootstrap once during first-time setup, and the role survived every subsequent restart — until `down -v` erased it.
- To make it work again right now: after the reset, re-run the bootstrap once — `psql "postgresql://cedar_owner:ownerpw@localhost:5432/cedar" -f db/bootstrap/create_app_role.sql`. To make it stay fixed: run the bootstrap from inside `reset-local-db.sh` (between `up` and `migrate.sh`), or mount the SQL into the container's `/docker-entrypoint-initdb.d/` so every fresh cluster creates the role automatically; either way, make the script idempotent (guard the `CREATE ROLE` so re-runs don't error) and update `docs/local-setup.md` to match.
report:
I have everything I need. The git history shows the repo has been in this state since its three initial commits — nothing was recently broken; the misconfiguration shipped with `CED-1` and the reset hazard with `CED-15`. Here is my report.

## Plan

Two reported symptoms in a multi-tenant clinic-scheduling backend: (a) `GET /clients` leaks rows across tenants despite tenant isolation in the schema, and (b) after a local DB wipe/rebuild, the dedicated application role no longer exists, so the isolation test suite fails at connect time. My plan was to locate the relevant project in the workspace, then trace the full chain for each symptom: what role the app actually connects as versus what role the isolation design expects (row-level-security bypass rules make this the prime suspect), and how the app role gets created versus what the reset script actually destroys and recreates. Everything was checked read-only: file reads and `git log`/`git status` only.

## Actions

1. Read `.claude/skills/environment-and-build-hazards/SKILL.md` (as instructed). Its "two-role model" section shaped the investigation: it flags that owner/superuser connections silently bypass row-level security, and that app-only roles created by one-time init scripts vanish when the data volume is reset.
2. `find`/`ls` on the workspace root and read the workspace `README.md` — the workspace holds many unrelated repos; `cedarline/` is the "clinic scheduling backend (local-dev tooling)" and is the only one with an isolation test suite, an app-role bootstrap, and reset tooling.
3. `find` on `cedarline/` — full file inventory (15 files).
4. Read `cedarline/README.md` — documents the one-time app-role bootstrap and the reset script.
5. Read `cedarline/.env` and `cedarline/.env.example` — both set `DATABASE_URL` to `cedar_owner`.
6. Read `cedarline/backend/app/db.py` — engine built directly from `DATABASE_URL`.
7. Read `cedarline/backend/app/deps.py` — per-request `SET app.current_tenant` on a pooled connection.
8. Read `cedarline/backend/app/main.py` — `GET /clients` uses `tenant_connection()` and selects from `clients` with no `WHERE` clause, relying entirely on RLS.
9. Read `cedarline/db/migrations/0001_init.sql` — RLS enabled (not forced) with `tenant_isolation` policies on `clients` and `appointments`, plus seed data for two tenants.
10. Read `cedarline/db/bootstrap/create_app_role.sql` — creates `cedar_app`; header says it is a one-time manual bootstrap, not part of automatic initialization.
11. Read `cedarline/docker-compose.yml` — Postgres 16 with `POSTGRES_USER: cedar_owner` and a named volume `cedar_pgdata`.
12. Read `cedarline/scripts/reset-local-db.sh` — `docker compose down -v` then `up` then `./scripts/migrate.sh`; never runs the bootstrap.
13. Read `cedarline/scripts/migrate.sh` — runs all migrations as `cedar_owner`.
14. Read `cedarline/backend/tests/test_isolation.py` and `cedarline/backend/tests/conftest.py` — suite connects as `cedar_app:apppw` via `TEST_DATABASE_URL` default.
15. Read `cedarline/docs/local-setup.md` — first-time setup lists the bootstrap as a manual one-time step; the reset section only mentions re-running migrations.
16. `git log --oneline --all --stat`, `git status`, `git branch -a` in `cedarline/` — three commits (CED-1, CED-7, CED-15), clean tree, single `main` branch; the current state is exactly as originally committed.
17. Read `cedarline/infra/main.tf` — a single Terraform variable, irrelevant to both issues.

## Answers

**1. Root cause of the cross-tenant leak: the application connects to Postgres as the schema-owning superuser (`cedar_owner`), and RLS does not apply to that role — so the isolation policies are silently skipped.**

The chain, with each link in a specific file:

- `cedarline/.env:1` (and `.env.example:1`) set `DATABASE_URL=postgresql://cedar_owner:ownerpw@localhost:5432/cedar`.
- `cedarline/backend/app/db.py:6` builds the SQLAlchemy engine directly from that `DATABASE_URL`, so every app connection is `cedar_owner`.
- `cedarline/docker-compose.yml:5` makes `cedar_owner` the `POSTGRES_USER` of the container. In the official Postgres image, `POSTGRES_USER` is the cluster **superuser**, and Postgres superusers bypass row-level security unconditionally.
- Independently, `cedarline/scripts/migrate.sh:4` runs all migrations as `cedar_owner`, so `cedar_owner` also **owns** the tables. `cedarline/db/migrations/0001_init.sql:21-27` uses `ALTER TABLE ... ENABLE ROW LEVEL SECURITY` but never `FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY` — and table owners bypass non-forced RLS. So even if `cedar_owner` were not a superuser, it would still bypass the policies.
- The endpoint depends entirely on RLS for filtering: `cedarline/backend/app/main.py:13` runs `SELECT id, tenant_id, name FROM clients` with no `WHERE tenant_id = ...`. The `SET app.current_tenant` in `cedarline/backend/app/deps.py:9-10` executes fine, but the `tenant_isolation` policy that would consume it (`0001_init.sql:22-23`) is never evaluated for a bypass-privileged connection. There is no error or warning — the policies just do nothing, and every tenant's rows (e.g. both seed tenants from `0001_init.sql:33-39`) come back.

The correctly-scoped role does exist in the design — `cedar_app` from `cedarline/db/bootstrap/create_app_role.sql` has only `SELECT/INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE` and no ownership or superuser bit — but nothing in the application ever uses it; only the test suite's default in `backend/tests/conftest.py:6` does. Git history confirms this was never wired up: `.env` has pointed at `cedar_owner` since the initial commit `a24f68f` (CED-1), and commit `03cd8a2` (CED-7) added `cedar_app` for the tests without repointing the app.

**2. The fix: connect the app as `cedar_app`, and keep two strictly separated roles going forward.**

- Change `DATABASE_URL` in `cedarline/.env` (and the template in `.env.example`) to `postgresql://cedar_app:apppw@localhost:5432/cedar`, so the engine in `backend/app/db.py` — and therefore `GET /clients` — runs as the least-privilege role that RLS actually applies to.
- Keep `cedar_owner` exclusively for schema changes: `scripts/migrate.sh` already hardcodes the owner URL, which is correct — migrations need ownership; the running app must never have it.
- As defense in depth, add `ALTER TABLE clients FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY;` (and the same for `appointments`) to the migrations, so even an accidental owner connection can't silently bypass the policies.
- Two adjacent weaknesses worth fixing at the same time: (a) `backend/app/deps.py` issues a session-level `SET app.current_tenant` on a connection drawn from SQLAlchemy's pool and `main.py` never closes it — the tenant setting can persist on the pooled connection and bleed into a later request for a different tenant; use `SET LOCAL` inside a transaction (or reset the GUC on connection check-in) instead. (b) The policy's `current_setting('app.current_tenant')` errors if the GUC was never set; `current_setting('app.current_tenant', true)` with a null-safe comparison fails closed more gracefully.
- Structurally going forward: application code paths get only the non-owning, non-superuser role with no `BYPASSRLS`; the owner role appears only in migration tooling; and an isolation test that queries as each tenant and asserts it sees only its own seed rows (the current `test_isolation.py:5-6` only asserts the URL contains `cedar_app`, which would not have caught this leak).

**3. The app role fails after a wipe because `cedar_app` lives in the cluster's role catalog inside the Docker volume that the reset script deletes, and nothing in the rebuild path recreates it.**

- Postgres roles are cluster-level objects stored in the data directory. `cedarline/docker-compose.yml:10-11` stores that data directory in the named volume `cedar_pgdata`.
- `cedarline/scripts/reset-local-db.sh:3` runs `docker compose down -v`, which removes that volume — destroying the entire cluster, including the `cedar_app` role. It then brings the container back up (a brand-new cluster containing only `cedar_owner`, recreated from the compose environment variables) and runs `./scripts/migrate.sh`, which only replays `db/migrations/*.sql` — schema and seed data, no roles.
- `cedar_app` is created solely by `cedarline/db/bootstrap/create_app_role.sql`, whose own header (lines 1–2) says it is a one-time manual bootstrap, "not part of automatic database initialization." `docs/local-setup.md` lists it only under "First time"; the "Reset your local db" section omits it entirely.
- The isolation suite's connection string defaults to `postgresql://cedar_app:apppw@localhost:5432/cedar` (`backend/tests/conftest.py:4-7`), so on the rebuilt cluster it fails immediately with Postgres's `FATAL: role "cedar_app" does not exist`. It "worked before the wipe" because the teammate had run the bootstrap once during first-time setup, and the role survived every subsequent restart — until `down -v` erased it.
- To make it work again right now: after the reset, re-run the bootstrap once — `psql "postgresql://cedar_owner:ownerpw@localhost:5432/cedar" -f db/bootstrap/create_app_role.sql`. To make it stay fixed: run the bootstrap from inside `reset-local-db.sh` (between `up` and `migrate.sh`), or mount the SQL into the container's `/docker-entrypoint-initdb.d/` so every fresh cluster creates the role automatically; either way, make the script idempotent (guard the `CREATE ROLE` so re-runs don't error) and update `docs/local-setup.md` to match.

## Next steps

If changes were allowed, in order:

1. Point `cedarline/.env` and `.env.example` at `cedar_app` and verify `GET /clients` then returns only the requesting tenant's seed row for each of the two seed tenants.
2. Add `FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY` for `clients` and `appointments` in a new migration.
3. Make `db/bootstrap/create_app_role.sql` idempotent and invoke it from `scripts/reset-local-db.sh` (or mount it into `/docker-entrypoint-initdb.d/`); update `docs/local-setup.md`'s reset section accordingly.
4. Fix the connection-scoped tenant GUC in `backend/app/deps.py` (`SET LOCAL` in a transaction, close/return connections) and make the policy null-safe.
5. Replace the string-matching test in `backend/tests/test_isolation.py` with real cross-tenant assertions against the seeded data.

## Confidence and gaps

- **Answer 1 (leak root cause): high confidence.** Every link is directly observable in the repo: the owner connection string in `.env`, the engine in `db.py`, the superuser-creating `POSTGRES_USER` in `docker-compose.yml`, owner-run migrations in `migrate.sh`, `ENABLE` (not `FORCE`) RLS in `0001_init.sql`, and the WHERE-less query in `main.py`. The bypass semantics (superuser always bypasses RLS; owners bypass unless forced) are standard Postgres behavior. The one thing I could not do read-only is reproduce the leak against a live database.
- **Answer 2 (fix): high confidence** in the role swap and two-role structure; the `deps.py` pooled-connection concern is a correct reading of the code but its real-world impact wasn't exercised.
- **Answer 3 (missing role): high confidence.** The `down -v` volume deletion, the manual one-time bootstrap, and the rebuild path that omits it are all explicit in `reset-local-db.sh`, `create_app_role.sql`, and `docs/local-setup.md`; the failing suite's dependence on `cedar_app` is explicit in `conftest.py`. The exact error text ("role does not exist") matches Postgres's behavior for a missing login role, though I did not reproduce it live.
- **Gaps:** I could not run the database, migrations, or tests (read-only constraint), so all conclusions are from static inspection. I did not examine the other workspace repos beyond identifying cedarline as the match; if the teammates' reports concerned a different repo, the analysis would need redoing — but cedarline is the only repo matching every detail (tenant-scoped `GET /clients`, RLS schema, dedicated app role, isolation suite, wipe-and-rebuild script).

The exact error text ("role does not exist") matches Postgres's behavior for a missing login role, though I did not reproduce it live.
- **Gaps:** I could not run the database, migrations, or tests (read-only constraint), so all conclusions are from static inspection. I did not examine the other workspace repos beyond identifying cedarline as the match; if the teammates' reports concerned a different repo, the analysis would need redoing — but cedarline is the only repo matching every detail (tenant-scoped `GET /clients`, RLS schema, dedicated app role, isolation suite, wipe-and-rebuild script).
sections.next steps:
If changes were allowed, in order:

1. Point `cedarline/.env` and `.env.example` at `cedar_app` and verify `GET /clients` then returns only the requesting tenant's seed row for each of the two seed tenants.
2. Add `FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY` for `clients` and `appointments` in a new migration.
3. Make `db/bootstrap/create_app_role.sql` idempotent and invoke it from `scripts/reset-local-db.sh` (or mount it into `/docker-entrypoint-initdb.d/`); update `docs/local-setup.md`'s reset section accordingly.
4. Fix the connection-scoped tenant GUC in `backend/app/deps.py` (`SET LOCAL` in a transaction, close/return connections) and make the policy null-safe.
5. Replace the string-matching test in `backend/tests/test_isolation.py` with real cross-tenant assertions against the seeded data.
sections.plan: Two reported symptoms in a multi-tenant clinic-scheduling backend: (a) `GET /clients` leaks rows across tenants despite tenant isolation in the schema, and (b) after a local DB wipe/rebuild, the dedicated application role no longer exists, so the isolation test suite fails at connect time. My plan was to locate the relevant project in the workspace, then trace the full chain for each symptom: what role the app actually connects as versus what role the isolation design expects (row-level-security bypass rules make this the prime suspect), and how the app role gets created versus what the reset script actually destroys and recreates. Everything was checked read-only: file reads and `git log`/`git status` only.
